text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Priya Shah  
Riverside Health Centre  
221 King Street, Hammersmith, London W6 9JT  

Dear Dr Priya Shah,                                                                                                         

Emily Carter, DOB: 23-11-1991, Hospital No: H673920 NHS No. 9843162754  
Flat 4, 8 Brook Mews, London W2 3BW  

Epilepsy 

Medication: Lamotrigine restarted January 2025  

Current regimen (patient-reported): Lamotrigine dispersible 50mg in the morning and 75mg at night. Adherence described as good; no missed doses in the last month. 

Lamotrigine Blood Level:  

15th September 2025: 3.2 mg/L (reference 1.0–15.0 mg/L). Dose at time of sampling: 50mg AM / 75mg PM. 

Seizures: Over the past six weeks, following a self-limiting viral illness with fever and cough in mid-August, there has been a clear worsening in events. The patient reports brief collapses with transient unresponsiveness occurring 2–3 times per week, some preceded by a sense of light-headedness and visual dimming, and others without warning. Features are mixed and not definitive for a single aetiology; events are currently best described as transient episodes with loss of awareness and occasional falls—unclear whether syncopal or epileptic in origin. There have been two nocturnal events witnessed by her partner with stiffening and tongue-biting reported, each lasting approximately 60–90 seconds, followed by confusion for up to 30 minutes. No clear triggers other than the recent viral illness have been identified. No head injury reported. 

I conducted a telephone review today. Since the viral illness, both frequency and severity of events have increased, with several near-miss injuries on stairs. She notes disturbed sleep, reduced appetite, and increased fatigue. She denies alcohol or recreational drug use. Menses regular. No new medications apart from a short course of paracetamol during the illness. Home blood pressure readings occasionally low-normal (systolic 95–105 mmHg). 

Plan: Given the recent deterioration in symptoms temporally related to the viral illness and the mixed semiology of events, I have arranged an urgent ambulatory EEG with ECG overlay and requested a 14-day event monitor via cardiology to assess for arrhythmic syncope. I have also requested repeat serum lamotrigine level, U&Es, LFTs, FBC, CRP, and ferritin, and asked her to maintain a detailed seizure and symptom diary noting posture, prodromes, duration, and recovery characteristics. Safety advice was reinforced (no driving, avoid heights, showers instead of baths, caution with hot surfaces). I will review results within 3–4 weeks and discuss with the multidisciplinary team. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: There have been two nocturnal events witnessed by her partner with stiffening and tongue-biting reported

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter describes two types of events: (1) brief collapses occurring 2–3 times per week but explicitly noted as unclear whether syncopal or epileptic; (2) two nocturnal events with classic epileptic features (stiffening, tongue-biting, post-ictal confusion). To determine seizure frequency, I prioritize definite/likely seizures. The two nocturnal events are the only clearly epileptic events, and they occurred within the period referenced ('over the past six weeks'). Therefore, the current seizure frequency is 2 events in 6 weeks. Format as '2 per 6 week' per the allowed output formats.
seizure_frequency_number: There have been two nocturnal events witnessed by her partner with stiffening and tongue-biting reported

flaws.description: Gold (2 per 6 week) disagrees with Reference (unknown)
